_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology per Sharma et al. Semin Arthritis Rheum 2020 (PMID 32521324) and Salvarani et al. NEJM 2014. Classification and management per 2022 ACR/EULAR classification criteria (Ponte et al. ARD 2022, PMID 35338033), 2018 EULAR GCA management recommendations, 2021 ACR/VF GCA guideline (Maz et al. Arthritis Care Res 2021, PMID 34235881). Tocilizumab evidence: GiACTA trial Stone NEJM 2017 (PMID 28723338). Imaging per EULAR ACR 2024 recommendations and TABUL (Luqmani et al. Health Technol Assess 2016).

h1Title: Giant Cell Arteritis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Giant cell arteritis is a large- and medium-vessel vasculitis affecting adults aged 50 and older, characterized by granulomatous inflammation of the aorta and its major branches, particularly the extracranial branches of the external carotid artery. Annual incidence is roughly 17-29 per 100,000 in Scandinavian populations aged 50 and older and lower in southern Europe, Asia, and Africa, with female-to-male ratio approximately 3:1. The classic presentation includes new headache (most often temporal), jaw claudication (pain in the masseter with chewing), scalp tenderness, polymyalgic shoulder and hip-girdle pain, low-grade fever, elevated ESR and CRP, and visual symptoms ranging from amaurosis fugax to permanent blindness from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy. Visual loss occurs in 15-20% of untreated cases and is largely preventable with prompt high-dose corticosteroid therapy. The 2022 ACR/EULAR classification criteria incorporate temporal artery biopsy, ultrasound, FDG-PET, MRI, and CT angiography. Treatment begins with high-dose prednisolone (40-60 mg/day, higher in visual involvement) tapered over 12-24 months, with tocilizumab (IL-6 receptor blocker) added as a steroid-sparing agent that reduces relapse and cumulative steroid dose.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual incidence 17-29 per 100,000 in adults aged 50+ in Scandinavia; 1-10 per 100,000 in southern Europe, North America, and Asia (Sharma 2020). Lifetime prevalence 0.1-0.3% in older Caucasian populations
keyStats.demographics: Female-to-male ratio approximately 3:1; rare under age 50; peak age 70-80 years; highest rates in populations of northern European descent
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at diagnosis 73 years; almost never occurs below age 50 (a criterion for the disease)
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 100,000-200,000 prevalent cases in the US; lower in absolute numbers but disproportionately high incidence in Scandinavian countries

definition: Giant cell arteritis (GCA, ICD-10: M31.5 GCA without polymyalgia rheumatica, M31.6 GCA with polymyalgia rheumatica, M31.7 anterior ischemic optic neuropathy associated) is a chronic granulomatous large- and medium-vessel vasculitis of adults aged 50 and older. The 2012 Chapel Hill Consensus Conference and 2022 ACR/EULAR classification criteria describe two overlapping phenotypes: cranial GCA, with predominant involvement of the extracranial branches of the external carotid artery (especially the superficial temporal, occipital, and posterior ciliary arteries), and large-vessel GCA, which involves the aorta and its major branches with axillary, subclavian, and aortic involvement detectable by imaging in 30-80% of patients. Polymyalgia rheumatica is a closely related condition affecting proximal muscle groups, with shared epidemiology and overlap in 40-60% of GCA patients. The pathological hallmark is transmural inflammation with mononuclear infiltrate, fragmentation of the internal elastic lamina, and granulomas often containing multinucleated giant cells. Diagnosis is supported by elevated ESR (often >50 mm/h) and CRP, temporal artery biopsy with characteristic findings, halo sign on temporal or axillary ultrasound, vessel-wall enhancement on MRI angiography, FDG uptake on PET-CT, and contrast enhancement on CT angiography. The 2022 ACR/EULAR criteria integrate clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings into a points-based classification system. GCA is managed primarily by rheumatologists, with input from ophthalmology, vascular surgery, neurology, and cardiology depending on disease pattern.

typesClassification.description: Predominant involvement of superficial temporal artery, occipital artery, and posterior ciliary arteries. Presents with new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and visual symptoms. Most amenable to temporal artery biopsy diagnosis. Highest risk of irreversible visual loss without treatment.

Predominant involvement of the aorta and its major branches (subclavian, axillary, vertebral). Detected by imaging (ultrasound, MR angiography, CT angiography, FDG-PET). Often presents with limb claudication, bruits, asymmetric blood pressure, or constitutional symptoms without prominent cranial features. Late complication of thoracic aortic aneurysm.
Common presentation; over 30-50% of cranial GCA cases have large-vessel involvement on imaging at diagnosis. Imaging confirms extent and guides surveillance for aortic aneurysm.

40-60% of GCA patients have concomitant PMR with proximal shoulder and hip-girdle pain and morning stiffness. PMR can precede, accompany, or follow GCA. Lower corticosteroid doses (15-25 mg/day) suffice for PMR-only, but GCA dosing predominates when both coexist.

Presents with unexplained fever, weight loss, fatigue, or anemia without prominent cranial or polymyalgic features. ESR and CRP elevation is the dominant finding. Imaging often required for diagnosis. Up to 5-10% of GCA presents this way.

primarySymptoms: New-onset, persistent headache, often unilateral and located over the temporal region, sometimes described as throbbing or boring; present in 70-80% of patients at diagnosis.
Scalp tenderness — discomfort when combing hair or wearing glasses — reflecting inflammation of branches of the external carotid artery.
Jaw claudication: pain or fatigue in the masseter muscle while chewing, relieved by rest. The most specific symptom for GCA, present in approximately 30-50% of cases.
Visual symptoms: amaurosis fugax (transient monocular vision loss lasting seconds to minutes), diplopia, partial or complete vision loss, often described as 'a curtain coming down' over the eye.
Anterior ischemic optic neuropathy producing sudden permanent unilateral blindness or severe visual loss; occurs in 15-20% of untreated cases.
Polymyalgic shoulder and hip-girdle pain with morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes, present in 40-60% of patients.
Constitutional symptoms: low-grade fever, fatigue, malaise, anorexia, and unexplained weight loss of 5-10% over weeks to months.
Tongue claudication or lingual pain on chewing, throat or oropharyngeal pain on swallowing; rare but highly specific findings.
Cough (often dry), upper respiratory symptoms without infection, and large-vessel symptoms (arm claudication, asymmetric pulses).
Stroke or transient ischemic attack from involvement of vertebral or cerebral arteries (uncommon, <5% of cases) — neurological emergency.
earlyWarningSigns: New persistent headache in an adult over 50, especially with scalp tenderness or worsening with brushing hair
Brief episodes of monocular vision loss lasting seconds to minutes (amaurosis fugax)
Fatigue or pain in the jaw with chewing that improves with rest
Aching and morning stiffness in the shoulders and hips suggesting polymyalgia rheumatica
Unexplained low-grade fever, weight loss, or anemia in an adult over 50 with elevated ESR or CRP
emergencySigns: Sudden permanent or partial vision loss in one eye — call emergency services and start high-dose corticosteroids urgently
New diplopia or visual field defect
New stroke or transient ischemic attack symptoms (unilateral weakness, slurred speech, facial droop)
Severe sudden chest pain, back pain, or hemodynamic instability suggesting aortic dissection or aneurysm rupture
Severe systemic deterioration with sepsis-like presentation in a known or suspected GCA patient on corticosteroids — consider opportunistic infection or steroid complication

causes.description: GCA is driven by activated T helper cells (Th1 and Th17) infiltrating arterial walls and producing IFN-γ, IL-17, IL-6, and other cytokines. Macrophages form multinucleated giant cells and granulomas. Vascular smooth-muscle and endothelial-cell responses produce intimal hyperplasia, internal elastic lamina fragmentation, and luminal narrowing or occlusion that drives ischemic symptoms.

GCA almost never occurs below age 50 and rises steeply thereafter. Age-related changes in immunity — clonal expansion of cytotoxic T cells, loss of regulatory T-cell function, and inflammaging — are leading hypotheses for the age-restricted onset.

HLA-DRB1*04 alleles (particularly *04:01 and *04:04) increase risk, similar to rheumatoid arthritis associations. Genome-wide studies identify additional susceptibility loci near PLG, P4HA2, and others. First-degree relatives have approximately 2-fold higher risk.

Seasonal patterns and clustering have been reported, raising the hypothesis of viral or bacterial triggers (parainfluenza, Mycoplasma, Chlamydia pneumoniae, parvovirus B19, varicella zoster); no organism has been consistently implicated. Recent VZV vasculopathy hypothesis remains debated; antiviral therapy has not been shown to alter outcomes.

Incidence is highest in northern European descent populations (Scandinavia, US Olmsted County) and lower in African, Asian, and Hispanic populations. Differences are partly genetic (HLA) but environmental contributions are likely as well.

riskFactors.description: By definition GCA does not occur below 50. Incidence rises steeply with age, peaking in the 70s-80s. Increases the importance of GCA in any older adult presenting with new headache or visual symptoms.
Female-to-male ratio approximately 3:1 (range 2-4:1 across cohorts). Female-skew is similar across most ethnic groups.
Highest rates in Scandinavian and northern European descent populations; lower in Asian, African, Middle Eastern, and Hispanic populations. Partially explained by HLA distribution.
Particularly *04:01 and *04:04 increase risk and are shared with rheumatoid arthritis. First-degree relatives have approximately 2-fold higher risk.
Approximately 10-20% of patients with isolated PMR develop GCA; 40-60% of GCA patients have concomitant PMR. They likely represent a spectrum of the same disease process.
Modest associations in some cohort studies; smoking may worsen cranial ischemic outcomes once GCA is established. Cardiovascular comorbidity affects treatment tolerance and outcomes.
Limited and inconsistent evidence for temporal association with infections or vaccines as triggers; no clinical action recommended on this basis.

affectedDemographics: Almost exclusively affects adults aged 50 and older, with peak incidence in the 70s-80s.
Northern European descent populations have the highest rates: 17-29 per 100,000 in Scandinavia compared with 1-10 per 100,000 in southern Europe, North America, and Asia.
Lifetime prevalence in older Caucasian populations 0.1-0.3%.
Substantially under-diagnosed in non-European populations partly because of physician unfamiliarity and partly because of true lower incidence.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical features, inflammatory markers, imaging, and biopsy according to the 2022 ACR/EULAR classification criteria. The clinical assessment focuses on the symptoms and signs of cranial and large-vessel disease, polymyalgic features, and constitutional symptoms. Laboratory testing nearly always shows elevated ESR (typically >50 mm/h, often 80-100+) and CRP (often >25 mg/L), with normocytic anemia, mild thrombocytosis, and modest liver enzyme elevation. ESR and CRP are normal in fewer than 5% of cases. The American College of Rheumatology and EULAR recommend imaging-first diagnostic strategies where expertise is available: temporal artery ultrasound demonstrating a non-compressible 'halo' sign (hypoechoic vessel wall thickening) is highly specific and increasingly used as a first-line confirmatory test. MR angiography of head, neck, and chest and FDG-PET-CT identify large-vessel involvement. Temporal artery biopsy remains the gold standard when imaging is unavailable or inconclusive, ideally performed within 14 days of starting corticosteroids and on a 1-2 cm segment of the symptomatic side. False negatives occur because of skip lesions; contralateral biopsy may be considered in suspicious cases with a negative first biopsy. The 2022 ACR/EULAR criteria require age ≥50, biopsy or imaging showing GCA, and the presence of additional clinical, laboratory, or imaging features summing to ≥6 points. Once diagnosis is made, baseline imaging from neck through chest is increasingly recommended to map large-vessel involvement and guide surveillance for aortic aneurysm, which develops in up to 18% of patients over 10 years.

diagnosticTests.test: Inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP, complete blood count, ferritin)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes systemic inflammation; ESR and CRP are typically markedly elevated in GCA
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw with results within 24 hours. ESR is usually 50-100 mm/h; CRP >25 mg/L. Normocytic anemia and mild thrombocytosis are common. Normal values are present in fewer than 5% of cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects non-compressible hypoechoic vessel-wall thickening (halo) characteristic of GCA; high specificity and sensitivity with experienced operators
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Office-based ultrasound of bilateral temporal and axillary arteries taking 20-30 minutes. Painless. Now recommended as first-line confirmatory test in centers with appropriate expertise per 2018 EULAR and 2021 ACR/VF guidelines.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Histopathological confirmation showing granulomatous inflammation, mononuclear infiltrate, fragmentation of internal elastic lamina, and multinucleated giant cells
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient surgical procedure under local anesthesia, removing a 1-2 cm segment of the symptomatic temporal artery. Heals over 1-2 weeks. Should ideally be performed within 14 days of starting corticosteroids; treatment must not be delayed pending biopsy.

diagnosticTests.test: MR angiography of head, neck, and chest
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies large-vessel involvement, mural thickening, enhancement, stenosis, aneurysm, and dissection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-60 minute MRI with gadolinium contrast through a small cannula. Particularly useful for non-cranial presentations and to guide surveillance for aortic aneurysm.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects metabolic activity consistent with large-vessel inflammation, particularly aortic and subclavian; useful for diagnosis of large-vessel GCA without prominent cranial features
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Whole-body or vascular-focused PET-CT with intravenous radiotracer; 30-60 minutes including uptake time. Best performed before or within 72 hours of starting corticosteroids to maximize sensitivity.

diagnosticTests.test: CT angiography of aorta and major branches
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies stenosis, aneurysm, dissection, and inflammatory wall thickening; alternative when MRI not feasible
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 5-15 minute CT with iodinated contrast; available widely. Provides good anatomical detail; less sensitive than MRI for early inflammatory wall changes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents fundoscopic findings of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (pale, swollen optic disc) and rules out other causes of visual loss
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Dilated fundoscopy by an ophthalmologist; same day evaluation is essential for any patient with visual symptoms. May include OCT and fluorescein angiography.

treatmentOverview: Treatment begins immediately when GCA is clinically suspected; diagnostic confirmation should not delay therapy because of the risk of irreversible visual loss. Initial induction is high-dose oral prednisolone or prednisone, typically 40-60 mg daily for uncomplicated cranial or large-vessel disease, or 1 g intravenous methylprednisolone daily for 3 days followed by oral prednisolone 60 mg daily for patients with visual involvement (anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, amaurosis fugax, diplopia). Symptomatic improvement (headache, scalp tenderness, polymyalgic pain) typically occurs within 24-72 hours; lack of response should prompt re-evaluation. After 2-4 weeks at induction dose, prednisone is tapered to 10-20 mg by 3 months, 7.5-10 mg by 6 months, and 5 mg or off by 12-24 months, guided by symptoms and inflammatory markers. The 2021 ACR/VF guideline and 2018 EULAR recommendations support adding a steroid-sparing agent — tocilizumab (IL-6 receptor antagonist) given subcutaneously 162 mg weekly or every two weeks — as initial co-therapy or for relapse, based on the GiACTA trial that showed tocilizumab with a 26-week prednisone taper produced higher sustained remission (56%) than placebo with a 52-week taper (14%) and reduced cumulative prednisone dose by approximately half. Methotrexate 7.5-25 mg weekly is an alternative steroid-sparing agent with modest evidence (relative risk reduction of relapse 0.65 in meta-analysis). Low-dose aspirin 75-100 mg daily is recommended for ischemic risk reduction unless contraindicated. Bone-protection measures (calcium 1000-1200 mg, vitamin D 800-2000 IU, bisphosphonate or denosumab in those with osteoporosis risk) are essential during prolonged glucocorticoid exposure. Pneumocystis prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is recommended for patients on >20 mg prednisone daily for >4 weeks combined with another immunosuppressant. Surveillance includes inflammatory markers, clinical symptoms, blood glucose, lipid profile, blood pressure, eye and visual-field examination, and imaging for large-vessel disease (annual aortic CT or MR for at least 5 years after diagnosis). Vaccination against influenza, pneumococcal pneumonia, herpes zoster (non-live recombinant), and SARS-CoV-2 is recommended.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisolone or prednisone (40-60 mg/day induction)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line induction therapy for uncomplicated GCA. Started immediately when GCA is clinically suspected. Tapered gradually over 12-24 months based on symptoms and inflammatory markers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic relief within 24-72 hours; prevents new ischemic events in the majority; without steroid-sparing co-therapy, 50-70% of patients experience at least one relapse during taper

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g daily for 3 days) for visual involvement
medicalTreatments.description: Pulse therapy reserved for amaurosis fugax, established anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, or progressive visual loss. Followed by oral prednisolone 60 mg daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: May stabilize and sometimes reverse acute visual loss in a small minority of cases; established vision loss usually remains permanent; reduces risk of contralateral eye involvement to <5%

medicalTreatments.name: Tocilizumab (162 mg subcutaneous weekly or every 2 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: IL-6 receptor antagonist used as steroid-sparing therapy in newly diagnosed and relapsing GCA. FDA approved for GCA based on the GiACTA trial.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: GiACTA trial: 56% sustained remission at 52 weeks with tocilizumab plus 26-week prednisone taper vs 14% with placebo plus 52-week taper; halves cumulative steroid exposure

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate (7.5-25 mg weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral or subcutaneous DMARD with modest evidence as steroid-sparing agent. Used when tocilizumab is unavailable, contraindicated, or has failed. Combined with folic acid supplementation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analysis suggests relative risk reduction of relapse approximately 0.65; less effective than tocilizumab in head-to-head comparisons

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose aspirin (75-100 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Recommended for vascular risk reduction in GCA unless contraindicated by bleeding risk. Considered in clinical guidelines to reduce ischemic events including stroke and visual loss.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational data suggest reduction in ischemic complications; evidence quality is moderate; widely used given low cost and acceptable safety profile

medicalTreatments.name: Bone-protection therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Calcium 1000-1200 mg/day, vitamin D 800-2000 IU/day, and bisphosphonate (alendronate 70 mg weekly, zoledronate annually) or denosumab in patients at high fracture risk. Started at initiation of corticosteroids for predicted long courses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces vertebral and hip fracture risk by 30-70% in glucocorticoid-treated patients; standard of care during long-term corticosteroid use

medicalTreatments.name: Pneumocystis pneumonia prophylaxis (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole)
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated for patients receiving prednisone >20 mg/day for >4 weeks plus another immunosuppressant. Reduces opportunistic infection risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents Pneumocystis pneumonia in 90%+ of treated cases; recommended in higher-risk combinations

surgicalOptions.name: Endovascular or surgical repair of aortic aneurysm
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for thoracic aortic aneurysm meeting size criteria (typically >5.5 cm) or rapid growth. GCA-related aneurysms develop in up to 18% of patients over 10 years and are a leading late cause of death.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-day mortality 1-5% in elective repair; substantially higher in emergent repair after rupture; long-term durability of endovascular repair excellent in selected patients

surgicalOptions.name: Vascular bypass or angioplasty for symptomatic large-vessel stenosis
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for severe symptomatic limb claudication, critical stenosis, or recurrent stroke from vertebral involvement that does not improve with medical therapy. Performed in remission to reduce restenosis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 70-80%; long-term patency depends on disease activity at intervention and continued immunosuppression

alternativeTreatments.name: Abatacept (T-cell co-stimulation modulator)
alternativeTreatments.description: Investigational steroid-sparing biologic with positive phase 2 evidence (Langford et al. ARTHRITIS RHEUMATOL 2017) for sustained remission. Not yet routine first-line but option in refractory cases at specialty centers.

alternativeTreatments.name: JAK inhibitors and other biologics
alternativeTreatments.description: Several agents (upadacitinib, sarilumab, secukinumab) in clinical trials. Patients with relapsing or refractory disease may be candidates for trial enrollment at academic vasculitis centers.

whySeeSpecialist: Rheumatologists with experience in vasculitis diagnose and manage GCA, individualize corticosteroid tapering, supervise steroid-sparing biologics, and surveil for large-vessel complications. Ophthalmology involvement is essential for any visual symptoms. Vascular surgery and cardiology contribute when aortic or limb arterial involvement is significant.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a rheumatologist with vasculitis experience at a center with temporal artery ultrasound, biopsy capability, MR or CT angiography, and access to tocilizumab. For visual symptoms, prefer immediate ophthalmology evaluation alongside rheumatology. Academic vasculitis centers offer trials and complex care for refractory disease.
hospitalCriteria: Vascular ultrasound capability with halo-sign expertise
Temporal artery biopsy and ophthalmology emergency evaluation
MR or CT angiography with vasculitis-appropriate protocols
Access to tocilizumab and other biologic agents
keyFacilities: Day-hospital infusion suites for biologic therapy
preventionStrategies: Recognize early symptoms (new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, visual symptoms) in adults over 50 to allow prompt evaluation and prevent vision loss.
Maintain regular cardiovascular care, including blood pressure, lipid, and diabetes management, especially during corticosteroid therapy.
Stop smoking, which may worsen ischemic outcomes in GCA.
Get age-appropriate vaccinations (pneumococcal, influenza, recombinant zoster, SARS-CoV-2) ideally before starting biologic therapy.
Maintain bone health with calcium, vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise, and DEXA screening every 1-2 years during corticosteroid therapy.
Communicate any new visual or vascular symptoms promptly during follow-up, including arm claudication, asymmetric blood pressure, and back pain.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere strictly to the prednisone taper schedule; do not stop or change dose without rheumatology guidance.
Take tocilizumab injections on schedule; report fever or infection promptly because of the IL-6 blockade-related risk.
Take calcium 1000-1200 mg, vitamin D 800-2000 IU daily, and any prescribed bisphosphonate.
Monitor blood pressure, blood sugar, and weight at home weekly during high-dose corticosteroid therapy.
Adopt anti-inflammatory dietary pattern (Mediterranean) to mitigate cardiovascular and metabolic effects of corticosteroids.
Maintain regular weight-bearing exercise (walking, light resistance training) to preserve bone and muscle during corticosteroid therapy.

recommended: Mediterranean-pattern diet to reduce cardiovascular risk and counter corticosteroid effects
Adequate calcium (1000-1200 mg/day) from dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens
Vitamin D 800-2000 IU/day through diet and supplementation
Lean protein at each meal (1-1.2 g/kg/day) to preserve muscle during corticosteroid therapy
avoid: Excessive sodium that worsens corticosteroid-induced hypertension
Refined sugars and simple carbohydrates that worsen steroid-induced hyperglycemia
Grapefruit, which interacts with some immunosuppressants
Excessive alcohol that worsens bone loss and hypertension

exerciseGuidelines: Maintain regular weight-bearing exercise (walking, light resistance training, balance exercises) throughout treatment. Avoid high-impact activities during prolonged high-dose corticosteroid therapy because of fracture risk. Physical therapy referral is reasonable for patients with polymyalgic shoulder and hip-girdle pain and for fall-prevention training.
dailyManagement: Take prednisone with food in the morning on schedule; do not skip or alter doses without rheumatology guidance.
Take tocilizumab subcutaneous injections on schedule and report fever or infection promptly.
Monitor blood pressure and blood sugar at home, especially during high-dose corticosteroid phases.
Maintain a written symptom log including headache, jaw pain, visual changes, and constitutional symptoms; share at each visit.
Attend regular follow-up at 2-4 weeks during induction, then 1-3 month intervals on maintenance, with annual aortic surveillance imaging.
Report any new visual symptoms, neurological deficits, or vascular pain immediately, even if mild.
prognosis: GCA mortality is generally similar to the age-matched general population when properly treated, but cardiovascular and cerebrovascular events are increased and aortic aneurysm and dissection are leading late causes of disease-related death. Visual loss occurs in 15-20% of untreated patients and falls to under 5% with prompt high-dose corticosteroid therapy; recovery of established vision loss is rare. Relapse occurs in 50-70% of patients during corticosteroid taper without steroid-sparing therapy and approximately 40% with tocilizumab co-therapy. Cumulative glucocorticoid-related morbidity (osteoporosis with fractures, infections, diabetes, hypertension, cataracts, weight gain, mood changes) is substantial and is the dominant long-term issue when GCA itself is controlled. Approximately 70% of patients ultimately achieve sustained remission off corticosteroids. Tocilizumab and other steroid-sparing strategies are extending steroid-free remission rates and reducing cumulative steroid exposure. Aortic aneurysm develops in up to 18% over 10 years; structured surveillance with annual imaging improves detection.
recoveryTimeline: Symptomatic improvement (headache, jaw pain, polymyalgia) within 24-72 hours of starting corticosteroids; inflammatory markers normalize over 2-6 weeks. Induction dose for 2-4 weeks; tapered to 10-20 mg by 3 months, 7.5-10 mg by 6 months, 5 mg or off by 12-24 months. Tocilizumab continues for 12-24 months in most protocols. Aortic surveillance imaging recommended annually for at least 5 years.
complications: Permanent visual loss (anterior ischemic optic neuropathy) in 15-20% of untreated patients; falls to <5% with prompt corticosteroids.
Stroke and transient ischemic attack, particularly from vertebral artery involvement (5% of cases).
Aortic aneurysm and dissection — develops in up to 18% over 10 years and is a leading late cause of disease-related death.
Glucocorticoid-induced complications: osteoporosis with fractures, hyperglycemia and diabetes, hypertension, weight gain, mood and sleep changes, cataracts, infection, adrenal suppression.
Tocilizumab-related complications: serious infections (especially diverticulitis perforation), neutropenia, hepatic transaminase elevation, hypertriglyceridemia.
Polymyalgia rheumatica relapse during steroid taper, distinct from GCA relapse but commonly occurring.

supportResources.name: Vasculitis Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient education, peer support, and clinical-trial information for GCA and other vasculitides; physician directory included.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — Patient Education on GCA
supportResources.description: Authoritative patient information and the 2021 ACR/VF GCA guideline summary.

supportResources.name: EULAR (European Alliance of Associations for Rheumatology) — Vasculitis Resources
supportResources.description: European clinical guidelines on GCA management and imaging.

supportResources.name: Arthritis Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient information on GCA, polymyalgia rheumatica, and steroid-related side effects.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polymyalgia Rheumatica
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) produces proximal shoulder and hip-girdle pain and stiffness without cranial features. PMR responds to lower steroid doses (15-25 mg/day vs 40-60 mg/day for GCA). However, 10-20% of PMR patients develop GCA, and 40-60% of GCA patients have concurrent PMR. They likely represent a spectrum of the same disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Migraine
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Migraine produces episodic headache, often with aura, photophobia, and family history, beginning in childhood through middle age. New persistent headache in an adult over 50 should not be attributed to migraine without excluding GCA, especially with constitutional symptoms or elevated inflammatory markers.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rheumatoid arthritis causes peripheral symmetric inflammatory arthritis and elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP, with characteristic erosive joint changes. GCA primarily affects large vessels and rarely produces peripheral arthritis. The two share HLA-DR4 associations and corticosteroid response but are distinguished by clinical pattern and imaging.

relatedConditions.name: Polymyalgia Rheumatica
relatedConditions.relevance: Closely related disease with substantial overlap; 40-60% of GCA patients have concurrent PMR and shared corticosteroid responsiveness.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shared HLA-DR4 associations; both are inflammatory autoimmune diseases of older adults, often managed by the same rheumatologist.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: GCA can cause stroke through vertebral artery involvement; differential and surveillance overlap.

relatedConditions.name: Migraine
relatedConditions.relevance: Common cause of headache that must be distinguished from GCA in older adults presenting with new headache.

relatedConditions.name: Mesothelioma
relatedConditions.relevance: Unrelated by pathology but listed because of similar emphasis on multidisciplinary care and concerning constitutional symptoms in older adults.

faqs.question: What is giant cell arteritis?
faqs.answer: Giant cell arteritis (GCA) is a large- and medium-vessel vasculitis affecting adults aged 50 and older. It causes granulomatous inflammation of arteries, particularly the temporal artery and its branches. Common features are new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, visual symptoms, and polymyalgic pain.

faqs.question: What is the difference between giant cell arteritis and temporal arteritis?
faqs.answer: Temporal arteritis is an older name that emphasized inflammation of the temporal artery. The current term, giant cell arteritis, reflects that the disease affects the aorta and many other large vessels in addition to the temporal artery and that giant cells are a histological hallmark.

faqs.question: Who gets giant cell arteritis?
faqs.answer: GCA almost exclusively affects adults aged 50 and older, with peak incidence in the 70s and 80s. It is approximately 3 times more common in women than men and substantially more common in populations of northern European ancestry. Annual incidence reaches 29 per 100,000 in Scandinavian countries.

faqs.question: Can giant cell arteritis cause blindness?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated GCA causes vision loss in 15-20% of patients, most often from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy with sudden permanent loss in one eye. Prompt high-dose corticosteroid therapy reduces this risk to under 5%. Vision loss already present is usually permanent; preventing the second eye is the priority.

faqs.question: How is giant cell arteritis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines clinical features, elevated ESR and CRP, temporal artery ultrasound (halo sign), temporal artery biopsy, and imaging of the aorta and large vessels (MR or CT angiography, FDG-PET). The 2022 ACR/EULAR criteria integrate these elements. Treatment should not be delayed pending biopsy.

faqs.question: What is the halo sign?
faqs.answer: The halo sign on temporal or axillary artery ultrasound is non-compressible hypoechoic thickening around the vessel wall, reflecting inflammation. It has high specificity and sensitivity for GCA in experienced hands and is now first-line confirmatory testing at many centers per EULAR and ACR/VF guidelines.

faqs.question: How is giant cell arteritis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment begins immediately with high-dose corticosteroids (prednisolone 40-60 mg/day, or 1 g IV methylprednisolone daily for 3 days in visual involvement) and is tapered over 12-24 months. Tocilizumab is added as a steroid-sparing biologic. Low-dose aspirin and bone protection are standard adjuncts.

faqs.question: How quickly do steroids work for GCA?
faqs.answer: Headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and polymyalgic pain typically improve within 24-72 hours of starting high-dose corticosteroids. Inflammatory markers normalize over 2-6 weeks. Lack of meaningful symptom response within 3 days should prompt re-evaluation for alternative diagnoses or complications.

faqs.question: What is tocilizumab and why is it used?
faqs.answer: Tocilizumab is a biologic that blocks the interleukin-6 receptor. It is approved for GCA based on the GiACTA trial, which showed substantially higher sustained remission rates (56% vs 14%) and roughly half the cumulative steroid dose when added to a corticosteroid taper. It is given subcutaneously weekly or every 2 weeks.

faqs.question: Will I need to take prednisone forever?
faqs.answer: Most patients require corticosteroids for 12-24 months. Approximately 70% achieve sustained remission off steroids long-term. Tocilizumab and methotrexate help reduce cumulative steroid exposure and prevent relapse. Some patients have a chronic relapsing course requiring low-dose maintenance therapy.

faqs.question: Can giant cell arteritis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Relapse occurs in 50-70% of patients during corticosteroid taper without steroid-sparing therapy and about 40% with tocilizumab. Relapses are usually managed by increasing prednisone temporarily and continuing or restarting tocilizumab. Relapse is not necessarily associated with permanent vision loss in patients under follow-up.

faqs.question: What is jaw claudication?
faqs.answer: Jaw claudication is pain or fatigue in the masseter (chewing) muscle that develops while chewing and resolves with rest. It reflects ischemia of facial artery branches and is one of the most specific symptoms of giant cell arteritis. Patients often describe it as 'having to stop chewing tough food'.

faqs.question: Do I need a temporal artery biopsy?
faqs.answer: Temporal artery biopsy remains the gold standard, but it is no longer always required when ultrasound shows a clear halo sign at experienced centers. Biopsy is still recommended when imaging is unavailable or inconclusive, or when the diagnosis is uncertain. It should ideally be performed within 14 days of starting steroids.

faqs.question: What is the link between GCA and polymyalgia rheumatica?
faqs.answer: Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) and GCA likely represent a spectrum of the same disease. About 40-60% of GCA patients have concurrent PMR features, and 10-20% of patients with isolated PMR later develop GCA. Both respond to corticosteroids but at different doses, and clinicians screen PMR patients for GCA features.

faqs.question: Can giant cell arteritis affect the heart and aorta?
faqs.answer: Yes. Up to 80% of GCA patients have large-vessel involvement on imaging, most often subclavian, axillary, and aorta. Aortic aneurysm develops in up to 18% over 10 years and dissection or rupture is a leading late cause of death. Annual surveillance imaging for at least 5 years is recommended.

faqs.question: Is GCA hereditary?
faqs.answer: GCA has a modest genetic component. First-degree relatives have approximately 2-fold higher risk. HLA-DRB1*04 alleles increase susceptibility. Most cases are sporadic; family history is not a strong individual risk factor compared with age and ethnic ancestry.

faqs.question: What side effects can I expect from prednisone?
faqs.answer: Common side effects include weight gain, hyperglycemia, hypertension, mood and sleep changes, insomnia, osteoporosis, cataracts, infection risk, and weakening of skin and bone. Bone-protection therapy, blood-pressure and glucose monitoring, and Pneumocystis prophylaxis in high-risk combinations help mitigate these effects.

faqs.question: Can GCA cause stroke?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 3-5% of GCA patients have stroke or transient ischemic attack, most often from vertebral artery involvement causing posterior circulation events. Stroke risk falls substantially with prompt treatment. New neurological symptoms in known GCA warrant urgent evaluation.

faqs.question: Should I take aspirin if I have GCA?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most cases. Low-dose aspirin 75-100 mg/day is recommended in GCA unless contraindicated by bleeding risk or other factors. Observational data suggest reduction in ischemic complications including stroke and visual events. Discuss with your rheumatologist and primary care clinician.

faqs.question: How often do I need follow-up for giant cell arteritis?
faqs.answer: Follow-up is every 2-4 weeks during induction, then every 1-3 months during the corticosteroid taper, and every 3-6 months long-term. Inflammatory markers, blood pressure, glucose, bone health, and vision are tracked. Annual imaging of the aorta is recommended for at least 5 years.

faqs.question: Is exercise safe during treatment for GCA?
faqs.answer: Yes. Regular weight-bearing exercise (walking, light resistance training, balance work) is encouraged throughout treatment. It helps preserve bone and muscle during corticosteroid therapy. Avoid high-impact activities during prolonged high-dose treatment because of osteoporotic fracture risk; physical therapy referral can tailor a safe program.
